segments.s178-g161: ز + noun here marks provenance ('from the treasure'); گنجش is the genitive/construct notion 'treasure/treasury'. نثار is a noun 'offering' used like 'offerings of…'. آورید is an archaic/persianate past (3rd pl.) form meaning 'they brought' (Ferdowsi uses -ید for past), so the clause reads 'from the treasure they brought offerings of peridot.'
segments.s178-g162: بسی is an adverb here meaning 'much/many'; گوهر is 'gem, jewel'; شاهوار is an adjectival epithet 'regal, lofty' modifying گوهر; آورید again is the past 3rd plural 'they brought'. Together: 'they brought many regal/lofty gems.'
segments.s178-g163: بسی qualifies آفرین as 'much/abundant praise'; بر + name (سیاوش) marks the target of the praise ('upon Siavash'); خواند is the narrative past (3rd sing.) 'he uttered/recited/said'. The clause therefore means 'he pronounced abundant praise upon Siavash.'
segments.s178-g164: که introduces a subordinate/casual clause ('for' / 'because'); خسرو here means 'the king'; به چهره is an idiomatic phrase 'in (outward) appearance'; جز او را is an excluding phrase 'no one except him' with را marking the object/affected; نماند is past (3rd sing.) negative sense 'remained/was left' — together: 'for the king had no one left like him in appearance.'
segments.s178-g165: ز پهلو is a prepositional phrase 'from the side/flank'; برفتند is past 3rd plural 'they went/departed/advanced'; آزادگان is the plural subject 'the free/noble ones' and follows the verb (inverted order common in epic style). The clause: 'from the flank the noblemen departed.'
segments.s178-g166: A nominal list apposing/expanding آزادگان: سپهبد, سران, و گرانمایگان — titles and epithets enumerated with no new verb (they are in apposition). This lists who the 'nobles' are: 'spahbeds, leaders, and the illustrious.'
segments.s178-g167: به شاهی is a goal/benefactive phrase 'to kingship' or 'for the king'; برو here functions as an exhortation/jussive sense within the acclamation formula ('may you go to kingship' ≈ 'hail/long live the king'); آفرین خواندند is past plural 'they uttered acclamations'. The whole is the crowd's acclamation: 'They cried "Hail to the king!"'.
segments.s178-g168: همه is the distributive determiner 'everyone/all'; زر و گوهر are the direct objects 'gold and jewels'; برافشاندند is past 3rd plural 'they scattered/showered' — 'all (together) scattered gold and gems' (i.e., they threw or showered them).
segments.s178-g89: برفتند is a past‑tense 3rd‑person plural verb ('they went/departed'). The noun دیوان (here 'the courtiers, the court') is the logical subject; the prepositional phrase به فرمان شاه ('by/at the king's command') gives the cause/agent. Note the classical/poetic ordering: verb قبل از فاعل (verb before subject) and the prepositional agent follows it.
segments.s178-g90: پدید آمد is a compound verbal idea meaning 'appeared/was made visible' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came'). در دژ is a locative phrase 'in/within the fortress', and از جایگاه indicates origin or location 'from/at the place' — the phrase order is poetic (source and location after the verb), so the whole clause reads 'a fortress came into view at/from that place.'
segments.s178-g91: به دژ در شد is an idiomatic classical expression meaning 'entered into the fortress': به ... شد gives the motion toward/into (به = 'into/towards'), در here functions as the notion of doorway/entrance, and شد is the past verb ('entered, became inside'). آن شاه آزادگان is a descriptive NP 'that king, the lord of the free (nobles)'; the order places the motion phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s178-g92: This segment lists proper names/titles who entered: 'ابا پیر' and 'گودرز' followed by 'کشوادگان' (a group/title). In this context ابا and پیر function as parts of personal names or titles rather than their common lexical senses, so they should be read as names; گودرز is the proper name Goudarz; کشوادگان is a collective/titleal word (archaic), here naming another party.
segments.s178-g93: یکی شهر دید is 'he saw a certain/a city' — یکی here functions as an indefinite/article ('a certain'), شهر is the direct object, and دید is the past verb 'saw'. اندر آن دژ فراخ is a locative description 'within that spacious fortress' (اندر = 'in/within', فراخ = 'spacious'), placed after the verb in classical order.
segments.s178-g94: پر از + noun means 'full of ...'. Here پر از باغ و میدان و ایوان و کاخ lists what the city is filled with: gardens (باغ), squares/fields (میدان), iwans/verandas (ایوان), and palaces (کاخ). The coordinating و links the items in a simple enumerative list.
segments.s178-g95: بدانجای کان is an initial adverbial phrase: بدانجا(ی) 'to/at that place/extent' (here used as 'at that moment/point') plus the archaic/poetic کان ('then/at that time' or indicating the past). روشنی is the noun 'light/brightness' and بردمید is a past verbal form meaning 'carried off/taken away' (third-person past); the clause literally says 'at that place/time the light was carried off'. Word order places the locative phrase up front for emphasis, then the noun (subject) and finally the past verb.
segments.s178-g96: سر باره forms an adverbial/idiomatic temporal phrase meaning 'on that occasion' or 'all at once/suddenly' (سر here heads the idiom; باره = 'occasion/instance'). دژ is the subject 'fortress/citadel', بشد is the past copular/auxiliary meaning 'it became/it happened', and ناپدید is a predicative adjective 'vanished/hidden'. The structure is S + V + predicate, yielding 'the fortress became (was) vanished/invisible'.
segments.s178-g9: به گرد اندرش = a prepositional phrase meaning 'around him.' Structure: به + گرد (ʼaround/aboutʼ) + اندرش, where اندر ('in/within') combines with the enclitic ـش (ʼhis/itsʼ). با درفش بنفش follows as an instrumental phrase ('with a purple banner'); in Persian the instrument marker با comes before the noun and the adjective بنفش follows the noun (درفش بنفش).
segments.s178-g10: به پا اندرون کرده زرینه کفش = 'he had put on golden shoes on his feet (inside).' به پا ('on the feet') is a locative/explanatory prepositional phrase; اندرون کرده is a perfect/participial construction (کرده, 'made/put on') that accompanies the object زرینه کفش ('golden shoes'). Adjective زرینه follows the noun کفش as usual; the whole phrase reports a completed (or stative) clothing action.
segments.s178-g11: جهانجوی بر تخت زرین نشست = subject (جهانجوی, 'the world-seeker') + locative phrase بر تخت زرین ('upon the golden throne') + verb نشست ('sat'). نشسـت is past tense 'sat' (acted/settled), and زرین is the adjective 'golden' placed after تخت as standard Persian word order.
segments.s178-g12: به سر برش تاجی و گرزی به دست = a verbless listing of his accoutrements: به سر ('on the head') + برش (here containing the enclitic ـش 'his' attached; the token is a classical/poetic sequence indicating 'his head') + تاجی ('a crown') and then a coordinated phrase و گرزی به دست ('and a mace in [his] hand'). The clause omits a finite verb and uses noun phrases to describe what he bore.
segments.s178-g13: دو یاره ز یاقوت و طوقی به زر = a list of ornaments: دو یاره (ʼtwo yārehʼ — a poetic name for ornaments) ز یاقوت ('made from/of ruby') and طوقی به زر ('a collar/necklace in gold'). The preposition ز is the poetic 'from/of' indicating material; طوقی is adjectival/nominal and به زر ('in/with gold') specifies the metal.
segments.s178-g14: به زر اندرون نقش کرده گهر = 'in the gold’s interior (به زر اندرون) gems were carved/inserted.' نقش کرده is a compound participial or perfect-like construction meaning 'had engraved/embossed/inlaid' (نقش کردن used transitively), and گهر ('gems') is the object. Word order places the locative/specification (به زر اندرون) before the verb phrase.
segments.s178-g15: همی رفت لشکر گروها گروه = an iterative/progressive past: همی + رفت gives 'kept going / marched on.' لشکر ('army') is followed by two nouns گروها گروه which list the army's formation as 'bands and companies' (reduplicative apposition/emphasis). The verb رفت is past; همی here conveys continuous or habitual motion in epic style.
segments.s178-g16: که از سم اسپان زمین شد چو کوه = relative/cause clause introduced by که ('so that/which'), where از سم اسپان ('from the hooves of the horses') gives the agent/cause and زمین شد چو کوه ('the ground became like a mountain') is the result: شد is the past of شدن 'became' and چو is the simile 'like/as'. سم means 'hoof' here and اسپان is the plural 'horses.'
segments.s178-g121: همان is a demonstrative here (’that/that very’) prefacing the personal name طوس; the phrase با کاویانی درفش is a prepositional accompaniment ('with the Kayanian banner'), where کاویانی modifies درفش (adjective before noun). The whole segment establishes the subject and his accompaniment.
segments.s178-g122: همی رفت combines the old/poetic particle همی (progressive/continuous aspect) with the past verb رفت (here functioning as a narrative 'was going' / 'marched'); با introduces another accompaniment phrase ('with drum and golden shoes'). و coordinates کوس and زرینه کفش, and زرینه is an adjective modifying کفش.
segments.s178-g123: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought'; the coordinating و links the actions. پیش جهاندار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of the sovereign' (پیش = before/in front of, جهاندار = sovereign). برد here functions as a past verb 'took/presented' (sequence of actions: brought and presented before the king).
segments.s178-g124: زمین را ببوسید is literal: زمین (earth/ground) marked by the object marker را, with ببوسید the past verb 'he kissed'. The following و او را سپرد links another past action: سپرد ('entrusted/handed over') with the pronoun او as its object (ambiguous in isolation—likely the banner or the thing brought).
segments.s178-g125: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him, a clitic/old dative form; گفت = said). کاین introduces reported speech or a content clause ('that this...'). کوس و زرینه کفش lists the items (کوس 'drum' coordinated with زرینه کفش 'golden shoes').
segments.s178-g126: به is dative/benefactive 'for/to'; نیک اختری is a compound adjective ('well-starred' → auspicious/luck-bringing), where نیک modifies اختری (star-like/stellar). کاویانی درفش is the noun phrase 'Kayanian banner' with کاویانی modifying درفش. The whole phrase assigns the items to that banner.
segments.s178-g127: ز لشکر ببین uses the poetic/prepositional ز = 'from/among' plus لشکر 'the army' and the imperative ببین 'see/look'. تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that/to see' and links to the following predicate سزاوار کیست ('who is worthy'). The clause functions as a command to examine the troops.
segments.s178-g128: یکی پهلوان is an indefinite noun phrase ('one/a certain champion'); از در کار is an idiomatic locution meaning 'in the matter/field of the affair' (از + در + کار = among/in regard to the task), and کیست is the copular interrogative ('who is'). Together it asks which particular hero is fit for the job.
segments.s178-g113: This is a noun phrase meaning 'all the chiefs' followed by manner/means. همه مهتران = 'all the great ones/chiefs' (مهتران is a plural). یک به یک is an idiom 'one by one' that modifies how they proceeded, and با نثار ('with offering(s)') states the accompaniment/means (با + noun). Word order is typical classical Persian: determiner/quantifier + head noun + adverbial phrase.
segments.s178-g114: برفتند is the 3rd person plural past of رفتن 'they went/advanced'. شادان is an adjective used adverbially 'joyful' describing their manner. بر before شهریار is the preposition 'toward/to' (an older/poetic form) yielding 'toward the king'; شهریار is the goal. The verb precedes the adverbial and prepositional phrase here, as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s178-g115: فریبرز is the personal name (subject). پیش آمدش combines the adverb/preposition پیش 'in the presence of/before' with آمد + enclitic ش (= 'to him'), so 'he came before him' or 'approached him'. با گروه adds 'with a company' (با + گروه). Note the enclitic object -ش attaches to آمد making آمدش.
segments.s178-g116: این عبارت gives origin and description: از ایران سپاهی = 'a troop from Iran' (از indicates origin; سپاهی is 'soldiers/troop' used attributively). بکردار is an adjectival form 'of good/strong bearing' modifying the force, and کوه 'mountain' is used as a simile/metaphor ('as a mountain'). Classical Persian often stacks modifiers after the head noun, so the phrase reads as an Iranian troop, virtuous/steadfast like a mountain.
segments.s178-g117: چو is the poetic/archaic subordinating conjunction 'when'. دیدش is دید 'saw' with the enclitic object -ش ('him'). فرود آمد is a compound meaning 'descended' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'), and از تخت زر is a prepositional phrase 'from the golden throne' (تخت 'throne' modified by زر 'gold'). The sequence places the temporal clause first: 'When he saw him, he came down from the golden throne.'
segments.s178-g118: ببوسید is the simple past 3rd person singular 'he kissed'. روی is the direct object 'face'; the following noun phrase برادر پدر is a genitive chain 'brother of (the) father' (i.e. 'father's brother' / 'uncle'). Persian often strings possessed nouns without a linking particle, so 'روی برادر پدر' = 'the face of the father's brother.'
segments.s178-g119: نشاندش is the transitive past form 'he seated him' (نشاند + enclitic -ش = 'him'). بر تخت زر شهریار is a noun phrase 'on the king's golden throne': بر = 'on', تخت = 'throne', زر = 'gold' (post‑nominal modifier), شهریار = 'king' (possessor/associative noun following the head). The object pronoun is attached to the verb as an enclitic.
segments.s178-g120: که introduces a relative clause modifying شهریار ('who'). بود is the past tense 'was'. از در here uses از + در to indicate association/origin ('from the court/house' in a figurative sense), یاره means 'attendants/followers' and گوشوار is a noun meaning 'jewelled ornament/earring'; together the clause says the king 'was (characterized) by attendants and ornaments' (i.e. decked out/surrounded by retinue and jewels).
segments.s178-g97: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb (literally 'he said/commanded') placed before the subject, as is common in classical Persian narrative. خسرو is the subject following the verb. بدان جایگاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/at that place' (بد- here is the old prepositional prefix + دان used adverbially) and completes the verbal idea 'ordered at that place'.
segments.s178-g98: یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective 'one' or 'a single', placed before the noun it modifies. گنبدی is an adjective/nominalized adjective 'domed' used here as a noun ('a dome'). تا ... سیاه expresses extent: تا ... به means 'as far as / reaching to', so تا + به + ابر سیاه = 'up to the black cloud'.
segments.s178-g99: درازی و پهنای او is a paired nominal phrase 'its length and breadth' where درازی and پهنای are head nouns joined by و. او is a possessive pronoun 'its' (referring back to the dome). کمند here follows as the measure/unit; the construction reads 'درازی و پهنای او ده کمند' = 'its length and width were ten (کمند)'.
segments.s178-g100: به گرد اندرش is a circumpositional phrase meaning 'around it' (به + گرد = 'around'; اندر + ش = 'in/within it' where ش is the enclitic possessive). طاقهای بلند is a noun phrase 'tall/high arches' (طاقهای = plural of طاق, بلند = high), which is located by the preceding circumpositional phrase.
segments.s178-g101: ز بیرون is the ablative/adverbial 'from outside'. دو نیمی (literally 'two halves') + تگ تازی اسپ is a compact descriptive group: نیمی indicates 'half/halves' and تگ likely qualifies the following تازی اسپ ('tazy asp' = Arabian horse/steed), so the phrase names the horses provided from outside (i.e., 'two half—(something) Arabian steeds'). Word order is noun modifiers before the head noun (تازی اسپ).
segments.s178-g102: برآورد و بنهاد are two coordinated past verbs: برآورد (here 'brought up/raised/produced') and بنهاد ('placed/installed' or 'founded'); together they describe two actions done by the agent (implied from context). آذرگشسپ is the direct object/name placed or installed. Classical Persian often uses verb1 و verb2 to list successive actions.
segments.s178-g103: نشستند is a past plural verb 'they sat' (verb before implicit subject-agent is normal in epic narrative). گرد اندرش is again 'around it' (گرد = around; اندر + ش = 'in/within it' with enclitic possessive). موبدان is the noun 'mobeds' (Zoroastrian priests) and functions as the subject/complements to the verb 'sat'.
segments.s178-g104: ستاره‌شناسان و هم بخردان lists two more groups: ستاره‌شناسان ('stargazers' — astronomers/astrologers) joined by و ('and') and هم بخردان ('also the wise men' — هم emphasises 'also/too'). Word order is noun – coordinating conjunction – modifier phrase, and all are nominative participants who 'sat' in the previous verb.
segments.s178-g169: جهان را is topicalized with the object marker را (marks the world as the theme). چنین است is a fixed copular phrase 'is thus/so', and ساز و نهاد are two coordinated nouns serving as the predicate meaning the world's 'making/structure and disposition/foundation'. Word order (Topic - Predicate) is typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s178-g170: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. یک دست is a bare noun phrase 'one hand'. بستد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular form (archaic -d ending) meaning 'bind/tie/seize' and pairs with بداد as a parallel verb; به دیگر introduces the beneficiary/recipient 'to another'. بداد here is a past verb 'gave' (3rd-person) used in parallel with بستد.
segments.s178-g171: بدردیم is 1st-person plural present/instantaneous sense 'we suffer / we are harmed' (classical ending -یم). ازین = از + این ('from this'); رفتن is a verbal noun (the act of 'going') and functions as the thing that causes the harm; اندر فریب = 'into/within deception' is a prepositional phrase showing where the 'going' leads.
segments.s178-g172: زمانی ... زمانی is a repetitive adverbial 'at one time ... at another time' (i.e., 'at times'). فراز = 'on high / ascent' and نشیب = 'descent / low point'; the two nouns form a standard antithetical pair meaning 'ups and downs'.
segments.s178-g173: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). دل is the logical subject/topic 'the heart'. توان here is the auxiliary 'can / be able to' and داشتن is the infinitival/gerundial 'to have/experience' (i.e., 'be able to have/feel'). شادمان is the predicate/adjective 'joyful'. Together they mean 'if the heart can be joyful'.
segments.s178-g174: به شادی = 'in/for joy' (prepositional phrase marking manner or domain). چرا is the interrogative 'why'. نگذرانی is 2nd-person singular present subjunctive/indicative with negation pattern here meaning 'do you not spend / why not spend' (literally 'do you not pass'), and زمان is the object 'time' — 'why not spend the time in joy.'
segments.s178-g175: به خوشی بناز is an imperative/advice: به + noun 'in/with happiness' + بناز (2nd-person imperative/present) 'take pride / rejoice'. و links to به خوبی ببخش where ببخش is an imperative 'grant/give/forgive' and به خوبی marks the manner/benefit 'with goodness' — 'rejoice in pleasure and give kindly.'
segments.s178-g176: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not'. روز را is the definite object 'the day' (را marks it). بر دل خویش is a prepositional phrase 'upon your own heart' (خویش = one's own; here split into خوی + ش where ش is the possessive element). رخش is a poetic noun 'beauty / countenance' (archaic), so the clause warns 'do not place the day's beauty/uplift upon your own heart' (i.e., do not let ephemeral day's splendor fix your heart).
segments.s178-g177: ترا (ت + را) is the accusative 'to/you' (ت as 2nd-person element plus the object marker را). داد is a past tense verb 'gave'. و links clauses 'and'. فرزند را is another definite object 'the child/son' (را marks it). هم is the adverb 'also/too' emphasizing that the child is an additional gift. دهد is a present/subjunctive (classical poetic) form used here to indicate a subsequent or prospective giving ('will give' / 'grants'); the clause thus strings a past action (داد) with a prospective one (دهد).
segments.s178-g178: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree' (with the -ی form). که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. از بیخ تو is a prepositional phrase 'from your root' (از = from, بیخ = root, تو = your). برجهد is a classical present/subjunctive/optative third-person singular meaning 'springs up' or 'will rise up'; it completes the relative clause describing the tree.
segments.s178-g179: نبینی is the negative present/optative form (ن + بیـنی) used rhetorically as 'do you not see' / 'you do not see'. که introduces the content clause 'that...'. گنجش (گنج) here means 'treasure/treasury'. پر از is the idiom 'full of'. خواستست is a fused classical form (خواست + است) functioning as the predicate 'is desire / is full of wants' — i.e. 'is filled with longing/want'.
segments.s178-g180: جهانی is the noun 'the world' (used as the subject/topic). به خوبی is an adverbial expression 'with goodness' or 'in goodness' (به used adverbially + خوبی 'goodness'). بیاراستست is a classical verbal/nominal form from آراستن meaning 'has been arrayed/ordered' or 'is adorned' — here it predicates that the world is arranged/adorned by goodness.
segments.s178-g181: کمی is the noun 'scarcity/little'. نیست is the existential negative 'is not' (there is not). در بخشش is a prepositional phrase 'in the giving/generosity' (در + بخشش). دادگر modifies بخشش: a genitival/appositive structure meaning 'of the just one' or 'the just ruler' — so the segment states 'there is no scarcity in the generosity of the just'.
segments.s178-g182: فزونی is 'abundance/excess'. بخوردست is a classical idiomatic predicate meaning 'it befits / it becomes' (literally related to 'belonging to eating' but here idiomatic: 'is fitting/appropriate'). انده (here an older form related to 'اندوه' / 'sorrow' in the poem's diction) followed by مخور — the negative imperative 'do not eat' (from خوردن) — yields the idiomatic admonition 'do not grieve' or 'do not be distressed'. Together the clause says roughly 'abundance is fitting — do not grieve.'
segments.s178-g49: Literal word order is somewhat inverted: 'یکی نیزه بگرفت خسرو به دست' = 'Khosrow took one spear into (his) hand.' بگرفت is the simple past (he took/seized). یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' modifying نیزه. به دست = 'into the hand / in hand' (به + noun giving a locative sense).
segments.s178-g50: همان نامه را is a definite object phrase ('that same letter' + object marker را). بر سر نیزه بست is a past tense clause: بر + سر = 'on/upon the head (tip)' of the spear, and بست (بستن) here means 'to bind/fasten' (he tied the letter on the spear's head). The segment describes the action completed by the subject.
segments.s178-g51: بسان introduces a simile ('like/as'). درفشی here denotes a banner/standard and acts as the object of the comparison. برآورد is a past-tense verb meaning 'he raised/put up' (archaic/composite), and راست is an adverbial adjective 'straight/upright' modifying how it was set up.
segments.s178-g52: به گیتی = 'in the world' (locative phrase). به جز introduces an exception ('except'), followed by فر یزدان 'the divine farr (royal radiance) of God' as the thing not excluded. نخواست is simple past negative 'did not want' / 'desired not' — idiomatically here: 'he desired nothing except ...' The subject is the same actor (Khosrow) from the preceding clause.
segments.s178-g53: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said (to do so)'. تا introduces the subordinate clause ('that / so that') giving the content of the order. گیو is the proper name (the addressee). با is the instrumental preposition 'with', and نیزه تفت is a nominal phrase 'a scorching/fiery spear' (تفت used adjectivally 'scorched, hot, blazing').
segments.s178-g54: به نزدیک آن = 'to/near that' (directional). بر شده is a compound/perfective participial idea 'having gone up / having advanced' (بر + شده), and باره رفت here describes the action of making an assault/charge and 'went' — باره has an idiomatic valence of an attack/occasion in this context and رفت is the past 'went' (he advanced/charged). Together they mean he moved forward close to it and made a charge.
segments.s178-g55: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'he said' — here گفت is past-tense reporting speech). کاین is the fused relative/ demonstrative 'that/this' introducing the noun phrase 'this letter', and پندمند is an adjective 'admonitory / full of counsel' describing the letter; the whole segment specifies which letter is to be taken.
segments.s178-g56: ببر is the imperative 'take!' (address to the addressee). سوی is a directional preposition 'toward(s)'. دیوار حصن is a genitive-like construction 'the wall of the fortress' (دیوار = wall, حصن = fortress), and بلند is a postpositive adjective 'high/lofty' modifying the wall. The clause is a direct command to carry the letter to the fortress's high wall.
segments.s178-g129: ز here is the causal/ablative preposition 'because of/for'; گفتارها is the plural 'words/speeches'; پوزش آورد is a light verbal phrase 'brought/produced an apology' (آورد is past stem used like 'brought/produced'); پیش here means 'before/in the presence of' (آورد پیش = 'brought forward, offered'). The segment therefore reads literally 'because of the words he brought an apology forward.'
segments.s178-g130: بپیچید is a past/preterite form meaning 'turned/twisted (away)' (the prefix ب- is an older verbal prefix retained in poetry); زان = 'from that/therefrom'; بیهده رای خویش = 'his vain/futile counsel' where رای = 'counsel/opinion' and خویش is the possessive ('his', here split into خوی + ش). The whole clause: 'he turned away from that futile counsel of his.'
segments.s178-g131: جهاندار پیروز is a nominal phrase 'the sovereign, victorious' (an epithet); بنواختش is a past form meaning 'struck him' or 'smote him' (نواخت as a past verb; the -ش suffix marks the object 'him'). The phrase thus names the actor and the action: 'the victorious sovereign struck him.'
segments.s178-g132: بخندید is the past tense 'he laughed'; و is the coordinating 'and'; بر تخت indicates location 'upon the throne' (بر + تخت); بنشاختش is a poetic past form meaning 'placed/seat(ed) him' (the -ش object suffix again). Together: 'he laughed and set him upon the throne.'
segments.s178-g133: بدو گفت is an inversion common in epic style: بدو = 'to him' (dative) and گفت = 'he said' (past). کین کاویانی درفش is a noun phrase 'the Kaviani banner of vengeance' with the genitive/attributive sequence 'کین (vengeance) + کاویانی (Kavian) + درفش (banner/standard)'. The speaker is thus indicating the emblem described.
segments.s178-g134: هم آن پهلوانی و زرینه کفش is a paratactic list: هم = 'also/too' or 'and', آن پهلوانی = 'that champion' (an epic nominal use), و = 'and', زرینه کفش = 'the golden shoes' (زرینه = 'golden', کفش = 'shoe/boot'). The clause is elliptical — a list of items the speaker indicates.
segments.s178-g135: نبینم is first-person negative present ('I do not see' — ن- negative + بینم 'I see'); سزای کسی = 'the desert/recompense of anyone' (سزا here = 'desert/recompense, worthiness'); در سپاه = 'in the army'. The clause states that the speaker sees no one in the host worthy (of the honor or post implied).
segments.s178-g136: ترا را here is the dative/accusative 'to you' formed from the archaic 2nd-person element ت + object marker را (together functioning as 'to you'); زیبد is a stative verb 'befits/suits' (classical present form); این کار و این دستگاه = 'this deed/affair and this arrangement/system'. The clause means 'this work and this arrangement befit you.'
segments.s178-g17: چو ... شد introduces a temporal subordinate clause: چو (when) + nominal phrase نزدیک دژ (near the fortress) + شد (past auxiliary 'became/came'). همی before the verb gives a classical narrative/continuative flavour ('thereupon/then') and برنشست is a past action verb 'sat up/ took a seat' (the subject is omitted and understood from context). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal 'چو' clause followed by the main action.
segments.s178-g18: This is a coordinated clause: بپوشید is a past tense verb 'he put on/wore' with its object درع (armor) immediately after it. The conjunction و links the next verbal clause, میان را ببست, where را is the definite object marker marking میان (here 'the girth / the middle strap') and ببست is the past verb 'he fastened/tied'. The verbs are simple past narrative forms.
segments.s178-g19: نویسنده‌ای خواست is a past transitive construction: an indefinite object/participant نویسنده‌ای ('a scribe / a certain scribe') with خواست 'he requested/desired'. The prepositional phrase بر پشت زین ('on/at the back of the saddle') indicates the place or the intended position (locative) and follows the verb phrase, as is common in Persian.
segments.s178-g20: یکی functions as an indefinite pronoun 'a certain (one)' and is the subject of فرمود here; نامه is the direct object. فرمود in classical usage can mean 'he said/dictated/caused to be made' — here 'composed/dictated a letter.' با آفرین is an adverbial phrase 'with "Afarin"' specifying the wording or opening formula of the letter.
segments.s178-g21: ز here is the poetic/instrumental preposition 'with / from' indicating the material or medium (in this context 'with amber/ambergris'), followed by the thing used (عنبر). نوشتند is the past plural 'they wrote/inscribed', and بر پهلوی ('on the flank/side') is a locative phrase introduced by بر. The clause thus states where and with what the inscription was made.
segments.s178-g22: چنان چون is an emphatic/discourse formula 'thus / such as', followed by the copula بود in the past 'was'; نامه خسروی is the noun phrase 'the royal/kingly letter' (خسروی qualifying نامه). The construction as a whole reports the content/form of the letter: 'thus was the king's letter.'
segments.s178-g23: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that / namely' identifying the sender. این نامه 'this letter' is the topic, از marks the source 'from', and بنده کردگار is a genitival phrase 'servant of the Creator' (بنده + کردگار). The clause states the origin of the letter.
segments.s178-g24: This is an appositive nominal string giving the sender's title and name: جهانجوی ('world-seeking', an epithet) + کیخسرو (the personal name) + نامدار ('renowned'). There is no copula; this simply names the author of the letter in classical Persian noun phrase order.
segments.s178-g81: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing the affected group; دیوان is the noun 'divs/demons' (here the victims). بسی شد is a quantified past construction: بسی ('many') + شد (past of شدن) yields 'many became/was' — شد functions as a past/copular/auxiliary forming the passive sense. به + پیکان marks the agent/instrument ('by arrows'); هلاک is a predicative adjective/participial noun meaning 'destroyed/slain'.
segments.s178-g82: This is a noun phrase with modifiers: بسی ('many') quantifies زهره (here used as a named/figurative noun); کفته is a past-participial adjective 'crushed/beat' modifying زهره; فتاده is another participial adjective 'fallen' that, with به خاک, completes the movement/result 'fallen to the ground.' Word order is noun + participles + prepositional goal.
segments.s178-g83: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that / thereupon'; یکی روشنی is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain light' (یکی = 'one/a certain', روشنی = 'light'); بردمید is the main verb in poetic diction meaning 'rose up/appeared' (an archaic/poetic perfective). The verb follows the noun phrase in typical Persian order (S V).
segments.s178-g84: شد is the past/copular verb 'became' introducing the result-state; آن تیرگی is the subject 'that darkness'; سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier 'completely / utterly' (literally 'head to head'); ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished / gone.' The clause means 'that darkness became wholly vanished.'
segments.s178-g85: جهان is the subject 'the world'; شد is the past 'became'; به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/likeness of' (کردار = 'manner, conduct'); تابنده ماه is a nominal phrase 'the shining moon' where تابنده is an adjective 'shining' qualifying ماه 'moon.' The clause reads 'the world became in the manner of the shining moon.'
segments.s178-g86: به نام is a prepositional phrase 'by/in the name (of)'; جهاندار is an honorific/epithet 'world-holder/sovereign'; پیروز is an epithet 'victorious' modifying شاه 'king.' The string is a titulary apposition: 'in the name (of) the sovereign, the victorious king.'
segments.s178-g87: برآمد is the perfect/past 'arose/issued forth'; یکی باد is an emphatic/indefinite noun phrase 'one/a certain wind'; با آفرین is an instrumental/accompanying phrase 'with afarin' — آفرین here functions as an exclamation or acclamation ('bravo'/'praise'), so the wind comes accompanied by that cry. Word order is verb — (indefinite) subject — adjunct.
segments.s178-g88: هوا is either 'air' or 'mood/atmosphere' and is the subject; گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' here meaning 'became'; خندان is the predicate adjective 'smiling/cheerful.' The conjunction و links the next phrase روی زمین, where روی means 'face' (the earth's face) so the line personifies the earth: 'the air became cheerful and the earth's face (too).' The verb is not repeated for the second clause (ellipsis of copula).
segments.s178-g33: جهانی سراسر is an emphatic noun phrase 'the whole world' (literal order noun + intensifier). به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'in/for kingship' (به used instrumentally/locative). مراست is the poetic fused form of مرا است ('mara ast' = 'is mine'); here it predicates possession: '...is mine (as king)'.
segments.s178-g34: در ... تا ... is a range expression: در گاو تا برج ماهی = 'from the Bull to the Fish' (zodiacal names). در marks location/constellation, تا gives the endpoint 'as far as', برج here means the zodiac sign, and مراست again = مرا است 'is mine' (copula fused).
segments.s178-g35: گر is the conditional 'if'. این دژ بر و بوم is a nominal phrase 'this citadel/fortress and its land' (بر و بوم is an idiom 'land, homeland'). آهرمنست is a contracted classical form = آهرمن است ('is of Ahriman' / 'belongs to Ahriman'); the predicate is fused onto the proper name.
segments.s178-g36: جهان‌آفرین را places 'the world‑creator' as a definite/affected element (را as object/topic marker); به دل 'in the heart' is a locative phrase; دشمنست is the fused form دشمن است ('is an enemy'). The construction literally reads 'X را به دل دشمن است' = 'X is an enemy in (the) heart', i.e. 'the Creator is regarded as an enemy in the heart.'
segments.s178-g37: Two parallel instrumental phrases introduced by به: به فر 'by (your) glory' (فر = royal/divine radiance) and به فرمان یزدان پاک 'and by the command of the pure God'. The second به takes the noun فرمان governed by the genitive/attributive phrase یزدان پاک.
segments.s178-g38: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely'. به گرز 'with the mace' marks the instrument. اندر آرم is a classical 1st‑person verb form (آرم = 'I put/place/bring' in older Persian; here volitional/resultative), and به خاک 'to the earth' gives the goal — together 'I will lay/bring (them) to the ground with the mace.'
segments.s178-g39: و‌گر = 'and if' (conditional introduction). جادوان is plural 'the magicians/sorcerers'. راست here is an adjective 'true' (post‑nominal), so جادوان راست این دستگاه = 'if the magicians' device is true' (این دستگاه = 'this device/contrivance').
segments.s178-g40: مرا خود is emphatic possessive 'my own' (مرا used classically for 'my' / 'me' with خود for emphasis). به جادو 'to/into magic' marks the recipient/instrument; نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not / should not'; سپاه 'army' placed at the end is the affected entity: literally 'my own (things) to magic must not — the army' → 'my own army must not be given to magic.' Note the omission/condensation of auxiliary/copular material typical of classical verse.
segments.s178-g65: شد is a past-tense copular/verb meaning “became” or “happened,” placed at the start in this poetic order. The following noun phrase آن نامه نامور ناپدید is the predicative phrase: آن (demonstrative) + نامه (noun) + نامور (adjective “renowned”) + ناپدید (adjective “vanished”), so the clause reads literally “became that renowned letter vanished” → “that renowned letter vanished.”
segments.s178-g66: خروش آمد is a simple past clause (“a roar came/rose”), with آمد as the past verb. و links a second clause; in خاک دژ بردمید the order is inverted: خاکِ دژ (“the dust of the fortress”) is the direct object/affected thing and بردمید is an older past verb meaning “carried off/was lifted,” so the sense is passive/affected—“the fortress’s dust was carried off / lifted up.”
segments.s178-g67: همانگه is a temporal adverb “at that moment.” به فرمان is a prepositional phrase where به is used in the sense “by/through” with فرمان (“command”), and یزدان پاک is the genitival specification “the pure God,” so the whole phrase means “by the command of the pure God.”
segments.s178-g68: ازان باره is an ablative/temporal phrase “from that occasion/thereupon.” The verb برآمد (“arose/emerged”) follows, and تراک (a fissure/crack) is the thing that comes into being; دژ indicates the location/origin (“from the fortress”). The clause therefore means “from that occasion a fissure arose from the fortress” → “a fissure opened in the fortress.”
segments.s178-g69: تو گفتی که is the reporting clause “you said that” (که introduces the subordinate clause). رعدست is an adjectival/predicative phrase meaning “it is thunderous” or “it is thunder-time,” and وقت بهار is a genitive/time phrase “the time of spring,” so together: “you said that it was the thunder-time of spring.”
segments.s178-g70: خروش آمد is again a simple past clause “a roar came.” The sources are given with two prepositional phrases joined by و: از دشت (“from the plain”) and ز کوهسار (poetic ザ = “from the mountain/ upland”), so “a roar came from the plain and from the mountain.”
segments.s178-g71: جهان گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن) to mean “became/turned” (the world took on a quality). چون introduces a simile (“like”), and روی زنگی سیاه is the compared image: روی (“face”) modified by زنگی سیاه (“a dark/Zangi black”), so “the world became like a black Zangi’s face.”
segments.s178-g72: The paired چه ... چه ... is correlative (“whether ... or .../be it ... or ...”). از باره دژ here means roughly “from the burst/assault of the fortress” (باره as a violent burst/occasion), and گرد سپاه means “the dust/cloud of the army” (گرد = dust). The whole line poses two possible sources of the darkness: the fortress’s burst or the host’s dust.
segments.s178-g41: چو here is the conditional/time conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. خم دوال کمند is a compact nominal phrase: خم 'a bend/coil' modified by دوال 'twofold, a pair' and then the head noun کمند 'lasso/snare' (so: 'two coils of the snare'). آورم is the 1st‑person present/imperative literary form 'I bring' (used here with future sense: 'I will cast/bring').
segments.s178-g42: سر جادوان را is the definite object (سر 'head' + جادوان 'magicians/enchanters', with the object marker را). به بند آورم is a verbal phrase in which به marks direction/into ('into') and بند آوردن is a compound meaning 'to put into fetters / to bind'. آورم is again 1st‑person 'I bring/put'.
segments.s178-g43: وگر is the conditional 'if'. خود here is an intensifier 'himself/the very', modifying خجسته 'blessed/auspicious' and the name سروش 'Soroush' (a divine messenger). اندرست is an archaic locative/copular form meaning 'is among / is within' (verb coming at clause end as in classical Persian).
segments.s178-g44: به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' where به has the instrumental/agentive sense 'by'. یکی لشکرست is the predicate: یکی 'one/a single' modifies لشکرست (لشکر + archaic copula -ست = 'is a host/army'). Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + predicate.
segments.s178-g45: همان من نه ... is a claused negation: همان 'just/indeed' + من 'I' and the negator نه (implied in the line) scope over the following prepositional phrase. از دست آهرمنم is the idiom 'from the hand/power of Ahriman' meaning 'under Ahriman's power' — here negated, so 'I am not at Ahriman's power.' آهرمنم names Ahriman (with the possessive/relational sense in the phrase).
segments.s178-g46: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for / because'. از فر means 'by/through the (divine) glory (فر)'; برزست is a past/classical verb 'was exalted/rose' and takes the noun phrase جان و تنم 'my soul and my body' as its subject/undergoer. The clause therefore explains the reason: 'for my soul and body were exalted by that glory.'
segments.s178-g47: به فرمان یزدان again is 'by the command of God' with به = 'by'. کند is the classical 3rd‑person singular present/past literary verb 'does/makes/acts'. این تهی is a nominal phrase 'this empty (one/thing)' (تهی here 'empty/vain') serving as the subject of کند: 'this empty thing acts by God's command.'
segments.s178-g48: که introduces the justificatory/subordinate clause 'that/for'. اینست is the copular phrase 'this is' and پیمان شاهنشهی is the predicate 'the covenant/pact of kingship' (شاهنشهی = imperial rule/kingship). The clause provides the reason: 'for this is the covenant of kingship.'
segments.s178-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when" here. آگاهی آمد is a two-word clause where آگاهی ("news/information") is the theme and آمد (past tense "came") is the verb; به آزادگان is a dative/prepositional phrase (به + noun) marking the recipients "to the nobles." Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal conjunction + (theme + verb) + indirect object.
segments.s178-g2: بر introduces a prepositional phrase attached to the prior verb phrase, here adding that the news fell "upon/to" a particular person. پیر گودرز is an appositive noun phrase "the old man Goudarz" (پیر = elderly), and کشوادگان follows as an epithet or group-name attached to/associated with Goudarz (an archaic/uncertain term in the text).
segments.s178-g3: که here functions as a connective/conjunction linking this clause to the previous one ("so/whereupon/that"). طوس و فریبرز are proper names joined by و. گشتند is the 3rd-person plural past of گشتن meaning "turned/changed (position)" and باز is a postverbal adverb meaning "back/again," giving the sense "they turned back."
segments.s178-g4: نیارست is a negative/periphrastic past modal here meaning "was not possible / could not" (it governs the following infinitive). رفتن is the infinitive "to go." بر introduces the goal/direction and دژ فراز is the goal: دژ "fortress/citadel" modified by فراز "high/above," so the clause means "could not go to the high fortress."
segments.s178-g5: بیاراست here is a past transitive verb "adorned/arrayed" (bi- prefix + arast), taking پیلان (elephants, plural) as its object. The clause then continues with و as a coordinator and برخاست غو where برخاست (past of برخاستن) is an intransitive verb "rose/started up" and غو is the noun "tumult/clamor," so "they adorned the elephants and a clamor arose."
segments.s178-g6: بیامد is the past tense "came/arrived." In classic Persian the verb may precede or follow its subject; here سپاه ("army/host") is the subject, modified by جهاندار نو = "of the sovereign / world-ruler" (جهاندار) and نو "new/fresh." The phrase therefore means "a new host of the sovereign arrived."
segments.s178-g7: یکی is an indefinite determiner "a/certain." تخت زرین is the noun + adjective "golden throne." زبرجدنگار is a compound adjective (زبرجد = peridot + نگار = inlaid/patterned) placed after the noun in the classical poetic style, so the whole phrase is "a golden throne inlaid with peridot(s)."
segments.s178-g8: نهاد here is the past of نهادن "placed/put (down)." از بر پیل is a classical locative formulation meaning "upon/on the elephant's back" (از + بر used idiomatically), and و بستند بار is coordinated: بستند (3rd pl. past of بستن) "they fastened/tied" with بار "load/cargo" as object. Together: "They placed [the throne] upon the elephant and they fastened the load."
segments.s178-g57: This is a sequence of imperatives. بنه is used as an imperative (here with the sense 'bundle/fasten the letter') and takes نامه as its direct object. The coordination و links that command to the second imperative بخوان, whose object is the noun phrase نامِ یزدان ('the name of God'). Word order is Verb + Object for the first verb and Object + Verb for the second (typical poetic flexibility).
segments.s178-g58: بگردان is an imperative 'turn!/urge!' commanding action toward عنان ('the reins'). تیز is an adjective modifying the implied motion of the reins ('swift'), and the coordinated phrase و لختی ممان is a prohibition: لختی 'a short while' + ممان (here used as a negative/withholding sense) yields 'do not pause/withhold even a little.' The sequence is imperative + modifier + coordinated negative admonition.
segments.s178-g59: بشد is a past/Perfect-type narrative verb ('it happened / he became') introducing the result; it combines with the following participial phrase نیزه گرفته به دست ('having taken a spear in (his) hand'). گرفته is a perfect participle describing گیو, and به دست marks the instrument/location ('in his hand' or 'into his hand').
segments.s178-g60: پر از is a predicative expression 'full of/of' followed by the content: آفرین جان (literally 'acclamations of the soul/life'). آفرین here means acclamations/praise and جان 'soul/life' — together indicating he is filled with shouts of praise; یزدان‌پرست is an adjectival epithet modifying the person (i.e., 'one who worships God'). Grammatically, پر از + noun(phrase) states what he is full of, and the final adjective describes him.
segments.s178-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is the direct object, and به دیوار دژ is the locative prepositional phrase 'on the wall of the fortress' (preposition + place noun). برنهاد is the past verb 'placed/raised upon' — so the clause reads 'when he placed the letter on the fortress wall.'
segments.s178-g62: به نام is the formula 'in the name (of)' marking the inscription; نام is the noun 'name' and the following compound جهانجوی خسرو نژاد is the name/epithet being given. جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeking' (qualifier of the name), خسرو is the proper name, and نژاد ('lineage') is an attributive noun indicating descent. The whole phrase functions as the content of the inscription.
segments.s178-g63: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the agent/source ('from the Creator'). The noun phrase دادار نیکی دهش literally 'the Creator's good gift' is the object of یاد کرد, where یاد کرد is a compound verb 'remembered / made mention (of)'. Word order places the source preposition first, then the possessed noun phrase, followed by the verb یاد کرد in past tense.
segments.s178-g64: پس 'then' introduces what follows. آن چرمه تیزرو is a noun phrase 'that (charger) — چرمه here a poetic term derived from leather/gear used to indicate a mount or its trappings — qualified by تیزرو 'swift'. باد کرد is a causative/simile construction: باد 'wind' + کرد 'made' = 'made (it) like the wind' or 'set it off with the speed of the wind' (i.e., sent the swifter-mounted horse away swiftly).
segments.s178-g153: وزانجا is a fused adverb meaning 'from there'; سوی is the preposition 'toward/to' and precedes the goal noun کاخ 'palace'. رفتند is the past plural verb 'they went'. باز is an adverb 'again' placed after the verb for emphasis—classical/poetic word order allows such placements.
segments.s178-g154: به marks direction 'to/onto' and governs تخت 'throne'. جهاندار is an epithet 'sovereign' modifying تخت; دیهیم ساز is a noun phrase where دیهیم 'rustic' qualifies ساز 'melody/instrument'. In classical poetry a nominal phrase like 'دیهیم ساز' can stand for the action 'a rustic tune was played' even though an explicit verb is omitted.
segments.s178-g155: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. کاووس is the subject; بر + تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the golden throne' (note adjective زرین follows the noun تخت in Persian). نشست is the past-tense verb 'sat' (classical/poetic simple past).
segments.s178-g156: گرفت is a past simple transitive 'he took'. آن زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at that time/then' inserted for emphasis. دستِ خسرو is the object 'Khosrow's hand' (possessive relation), and the phrase به دست here is idiomatic 'by the hand' meaning 'took (him) by the hand.' The actual subject (he, i.e. Kavus) is implied from the previous clause.
segments.s178-g157: بیاورد (here) is used as a past-tense verb 'he brought'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بنشاند is an archaic/poetic causative past meaning 'he seated (him)' or 'set (him) down' (note this is a literary form). بر جای خویش = 'on his own seat/place' with خویش split into خوی + ش (possessive clitic).
segments.s178-g158: ز گنجور is a prepositional phrase 'from the treasury' (ز = from, گنجور = treasury). تاج کیان is 'the crown of the Kians/kings' with کیان governing تاج. خواست is the past verb 'he asked/demanded/wanted' and پیش functions adverbially 'before/in the presence of' (i.e. 'to be brought before him'). The clause is compact and typical of epic diction.
segments.s178-g159: ببوسید is past 'he kissed (him)'. و links two actions. بنهاد is a literary past meaning 'he put/placed' (here 'placed the crown'). بر سرش is the locative 'upon his head' with the possessive clitic ش attached to سر. تاج is the direct object 'crown.'
segments.s178-g160: به کرسی شد is an idiom meaning 'he took/assumed the seat' (به + noun + شد where شد is 'became/was seated'). از نامور تخت عاج is a descriptive phrase: نامور 'renowned' modifies تخت عاج 'ivory throne'; از here introduces the famed/characteristic throne (poetic usage rather than strictly 'from'). Overall it states that he sat upon the famed ivory throne.
segments.s178-g73: تو گفتی is a past-tense statement 'you said' (تو + گفتی, 2nd-person singular past). The clause that follows — برآمد یکی تیره ابر — is reported content (the thing said) with a verb-initial, poetic order: برآمد (arose) + یکی (a/one) + تیره ابر (dark cloud). Persian narrative often allows the verb before its nominal phrase in older/epic style.
segments.s178-g74: هوا شد is 'the air/atmosphere became' with شد as the simple past/perfective of شدن used to mark change of state. به کردار کام هژبر is a prepositional phrase expressing manner: به + کردار ('in the manner/behavior') + کام هژبر ('Hojabr's desire'). Note the poetic omission of the expected ezāfe (e.g. کامِ هژبر) — nouns are juxtaposed without the linking -e in epic Persian.
segments.s178-g75: برانگیخت is a past transitive verb 'he roused/raised' (here acting on a horse). The following noun kiхسرو is the subject in V‑S order (typical poetic inversion), and اسپ سیاه is the object 'the black steed' (adjective after the noun is normal in older Persian poetry).
segments.s178-g76: چنین گفت is the narrative formula 'thus he said' (چنین = thus, گفت = 'said', 3rd sing. past). با پهلوان سپاه is an oblique phrase introduced by با ('with/ to'), here meaning 'to/with the champion of the host' — note again the compressed noun sequence پهلوان سپاه ('champion of the army') without an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s178-g77: که introduces the content of what was said (subordinate clause). بر دژ is a prepositional phrase 'upon the fortress' placed before the object; یکی تیر باران کنید is an imperative/subjunctive addressed to multiple addressees: کنید is 2nd-person plural present-subjunctive/imperative 'do/make', تیر باران 'shower of arrows' uses poetic noun stacking and یکی functions as an indefinite/quantifying element ('a certain' / 'a volley of').
segments.s178-g78: هوا را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the air' as direct object). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', so چو ابر بهاران کن��د is a second coordinated imperative 'make the air like spring clouds'; ابر بهاران is a compact noun phrase 'spring-cloud(s)' without ezāfe, and کنید again marks the plural command.
segments.s178-g79: برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared'. یکی introduces the following nominal group as indefinite ('a certain' / 'one'), and میغ بار + ش (بار + ش = بارش) is a poetic compound: میغ 'blade' modifies بارش 'fall/shower' (بار + enclitic ش = its falling), so میغ بارش تگرگ describes a hail-fall whose hail are like blades. Verb-before-nominal order and the enclitic ش forming بارش are characteristic of the poetic register.
segments.s178-g80: تگرگی is a nominal (indefinite) 'a hail/ a hailstorm' followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('that/which'). بردارد is a present/subjunctive/potential verb here 'would take/bring' describing the hail's effect, and از ابر مرگ is the prepositional phrase 'from the cloud of death' (ابر مرگ is compact, again without ezāfe). The relative clause gives the lethal quality of the hail.
segments.s178-g25: که is the relative 'who/that' introducing this clause. از + بند = 'from the bond'; بند here means a fetter/bond. آهرمن بد places the epithet بد ('evil') after the proper name Ahriman (poetic order) = 'Ahriman the evil'. بجست is an older/poetic past form (بجهیدن/جهیدن) meaning 'leapt away' or 'escaped' (3rd sing. past).
segments.s178-g26: به یزدان زد is an idiomatic classical use: به + یزدان = 'to/unto God'; زد (literally 'struck') is used here intransitively/idiomatically to mean 'betook himself to' or 'turned to' (poetic usage). از هر بدی پاک دست is a prepositional phrase 'free from every evil' where از expresses separation, هر بدی = 'every evil', and پاکِ دست ('clean-handed') is a compound adjectival phrase describing the subject (hands clean = innocent).
segments.s178-g27: که again introduces the clause. اویست is a contracted classical form (او + است) meaning 'he is'—here functioning as the copula (note: the provided lexical senses refer to a proper name but the form functions as 'اوست'). جاوید 'eternal' and برتر 'higher/supreme' are predicative adjectives placed after the copula; خدای is the noun 'God' (predicate nominal).
segments.s178-g28: خداوند is vocative/invocational 'the Lord/God'. نیکی ده is an imperative/request: نیکی (goodness) + ده (imperative of دادن) = 'give goodness' or 'grant goodness'. و = 'and'. رهنمای here functions as a noun 'guide' (understood elliptically as 'be (our) guide' or 'grant guidance').
segments.s178-g29: This is an enumerative invocation: خداوند 'the Lord' followed by three names/titles joined by و. بهرام, کیوان, هور are proper names/astrological/mythical epithets invoked to strengthen the prayer (classical poetic names — Bahram often Mars/war, Kiwan/Saturn, Hoor = splendour or solar epithet).
segments.s178-g30: Another appositive series: خداوند فر = 'the Lord of far (farr, royal glory)' and خداوند زور = 'the Lord of strength/might'. فر is the technical Old/Iranian concept of royal glory or divine charisma; زور is 'power/strength'. The repetition of خداوند is an emphatic, honorific construction.
segments.s178-g31: مرا is the object pronoun 'me' (م + را combined: enclitic pronoun + object marker). داد is the past 'gave'. اورند here is actually the archaic/poetic 3rd‑pl. form (آورند/آوردند) 'they brought/they gave' in context, though the provided lemma senses list does not reflect that; و = 'and'. فر کیان = 'the farr of the Kians' (فر = royal glory, کیان = the Kayanian dynasty). The clause therefore states that (they) gave/granted the speaker the Kayanian farr.
segments.s178-g32: This is a series of nominal comparisons in juxtaposition: تن پیل = 'a body of/like an elephant' (تن 'body' + پیل 'elephant'), و = 'and', چنگال شیر = 'the claws of a lion' (possessive/genitive linkage by noun order). ژیان ('life') follows and intensifies/lends vividness ('living'/'vital') in poetic diction. The structure lists bestowed physical/heroic attributes.
segments.s178-g105: دران is a locative adverb ('in that'); شارستان is the place noun 'city'. Classical Persian often uses noun + verb order, so کرد (literally 'made/did') combines with the noun درنگ to form a verbal expression 'made a delay' = 'tarried'. چنان/چندان is a degree adverb modifying درنگ ('to such an extent/a long while'). The whole segment = 'in that city he tarried so long'.
segments.s178-g106: که introduces a result clause ('so that/that'); آتشکده is the subject 'the fire-temple'. گشت is the past of گشتن used here as 'became/was turned into'. با + nouns indicates accompaniment/being filled with, so با بوی و رنگ means 'with scent and colour'. Together: '...so that the fire-temple became filled with scent and colour.'
segments.s178-g107: چو یک سال بگذشت is a temporal clause ('when a year passed'); چو = 'when', یک سال = 'one year', بگذشت = past 'passed'. After the temporal clause the main clause begins: لشکر (the army) + براند (classical verb meaning 'drove/advanced/set in motion'), so 'the army was set in motion' / 'he put the army on the move.'
segments.s178-g108: This is a coordination of two past actions. بنه here = 'baggage/bundle'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' → 'put the baggage on (the beasts/wagons)'. و links the next clause: سپه = 'the host/army' and برنشاند (causative/past) = 'set forth/seated/arranged' → 'marshalled the host' or 'set the army in order.'
segments.s178-g109: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); آگاهی ('information/word') is the subject that آمد ('came'). به ایران marks the destination/recipient 'to Iran' and ز شاه gives the source 'from the king'. The clause reads 'when information came to Iran from the king'.
segments.s178-g110: This is a noun phrase specifying the content of the news: ازان here functions as a genitive/relational 'of that' (i.e. 'about that'). ایزدی qualifies فر ('divine' radiance/glory) and و آن دستگاه ('and that court/establishment') completes the apposition. So: 'news ... of that divine farr and that court.'
segments.s178-g111: جهانی ('the whole world' or 'all') is the subject. فرو ماند is a compound: فرو (an intensifier/adverb 'down/deeply') + ماند (past of ماندن 'remained') → 'was left/was cast (deeply)'. اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment'. Together: 'the whole world was left in deep wonder.'
segments.s178-g112: که introduces the explanatory/cause clause ('that/for'); کیخسرو is the subject. آن فر ('that farr' = that royal/divine glory) is the object, و links the phrase to بالا گرفت, where بالا (up/high) + گرفت (past of گرفتن 'took/seized') together mean 'took up/raised on high'. So: 'that Kay Khosrow seized that glory and raised it up.'
segments.s178-g137: ترا (ت + را) is the dative/accusative clitic 'to/for you' placed before the predicate. پوزش 'apology' is the noun head; اکنون 'now' is a temporal adverb placed after the noun. نیاید is an archaic/poetic present/past-form of آمدن with negative sense ('does not come' / 'will not come'), used here in the idiom نیاید به کار ('does not come to use') meaning 'is of no use'. به کار is the fixed prepositional-complement meaning 'to use / for use' and together نیاید به کار = 'will not be useful.'
segments.s178-g138: نه is the clause-negator linking this clause to the previous one ('nor / and not'). بیگانه‌ای is an indefinite-form of بیگانه ('a stranger'); خواستی is 2nd-person past 'you wanted' (خواستن in the past); شهریار at the end is a vocative ('O king') addressing the addressee. Word order is object (بیگانه‌ای) + verb (خواستی) + vocative (شهریار), a common classical Persian arrangement.
segments.s178-g139: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. پیروز is an adjectival / predicative element 'victorious' and combines with برگشت (past 'returned') to mean 'returned victorious.' The verb برگشت (past of برگشتن) precedes the subject شیر here (verb–subject order), which is common in classical Persian. از نبرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the battle' marking source/place.
segments.s178-g140: دل و دیده is a coordinated object ('heart and eye(s)'). دشمنان follows as the possessor ('of the enemies') — Persian places the possessed before the possessor. تیره is an adjective 'dark/bleak' placed before the verbal predicate; کرد is the past-tense of کردن used here causatively ('made/caused to be'). So تیره کرد = 'caused (them) to be dark' (i.e. filled them with gloom).
segments.s178-g141: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'; پهلو 'flank/side' is its object. پارس here stands as a genitive/possessive noun ('of Pars' / 'Pars' side'). بنهاد is an archaic past form of نهادن ('he set/placed/turned'); it governs the object روی 'face' (بنهاد روی = 'turned/set his face toward'). The order is adverbial phrase (سوی پهلو پارس) + verb (بنهاد) + object (روی).
segments.s178-g142: جوان بود is a simple copular clause ('he was young'): بود is past-tense copula. The adjectives/participial phrases are conjoined with و: بیدار 'awake/alert' and the compound دیهیم جوی where دیهیم ('rural, rustic') and جوی ('seeking' / 'searching') form a descriptive phrase modifying the same subject. In short: 'he was young and awake and a seeker of (the) rural' — i.e. 'young, alert, and eager in his search.'
segments.s178-g143: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ز + و here represent the older contracted z + u ('from him' / 'from it'); the phrase زو (written here as ز و) means 'from him' or 'from it'. آگهی یافت is a past compound meaning 'obtained information / learned' (یافت = 'found/obtained'); کاووس is the subject placed after the verb in V–S order. کی is an interrogative/relative ('who' / 'when') that links forward to the relative clause that follows.
segments.s178-g144: که is the relative/complementizer ('that/who') introducing the subordinate clause. آمد is the simple past 'came'. ز ره is the prepositional phrase 'by/from the road' (ز = 'from', ره = 'way/road'). پور is the element 'pour / son' (here part of a name or patronymic), فرخنده may be a proper-name element ('Farrokhandeh' / 'auspicious') and پی here carries the sense 'in pursuit of / following' — together forming a phrase indicating who was being followed or whom the newcomer was following (literal: 'came from the road following Pour‑Farrokhandeh').
segments.s178-g151: بخندید is the simple past (3rd person singular) of خندیدن — "he laughed/ smiled." و is the coordinating conjunction linking two actions. او را is the direct object (او = him/her; را = definite object marker) placed before the verb as is normal in Persian word order. به + (بر در) is a prepositional phrase giving direction — به here means "toward/into." گرفت is the past (3rd sg.) of گرفتن meaning "took/led/seized," so the whole clause reads literally "he smiled and took him toward the door/doorway."
segments.s178-g152: نیایش is the noun "prayer." سزاوار is an adjective meaning "befitting/appropriate" that follows the noun here and is tied to او ("for him"); the phrase نیایش سزاوار او therefore means "a prayer appropriate for him." برگرفت is the past (3rd sg.) of برگرفتن used here in the sense "took up / raised / began" (i.e., to take up a prayer = to intone or offer it).
segments.s178-g145: پذیره شدش is a passive or stative expression meaning 'he/it was received' or 'he was welcomed' — شدش is the clitic-attached past auxiliary indicating the event happened to him. با رخی ارغوان is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with a crimson/rosy face' (رخی = 'a face/complexion' formed from رخ, and ارغوان modifies it). Note the adjective ارغوان follows the noun رخی, a frequent order in classical Persian.
segments.s178-g146: ز شادی is an agentive/causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of joy'. دل is the noun 'heart'. پیر گشته is a participial construction: پیر (old) + گشته (perfect/participial 'become'), together 'having become old' or 'grown old'. جوان follows as the resulting descriptor 'young'. In this poetic verbless clause the sense is 'from joy, the heart that had become old turned/become young.'
segments.s178-g147: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'. خسرو is the subject (the name 'Khosrow'), نیا را is the definite/direct object marked by را, and بدید is the simple past 'saw'. Word order is typical for classical Persian: temporal conjunction + adverbial + subject + object (with را) + verb.
segments.s178-g148: بخندید is the simple past 'he laughed'. و links the coordinated action. شادان functions as a predicative/adverbial adjective 'joyful' (describing the state in which the next action occurs). دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his). بردمید is the past verb 'carried off/seized' used figuratively here ('his heart was carried away' = he was thrilled). The clause reads 'he laughed and, joyful, his heart was carried away.'
segments.s178-g149: پیاده شد is an idiom 'he dismounted / alighted' literally 'became on foot' (پیاده = on foot). و coordinates the next action. برد پیشش نماز is 'he carried/brought prayer before him' where برد is the past of بردن 'to carry/bring', پیشش = 'in his presence/before him' and نماز is the object 'prayer' (here likely meaning he presented or performed a prayer in the addressee's presence).
segments.s178-g150: به دیدار او is a purpose / directional phrase 'to meet him' or 'for a meeting with him' (به = to/for, دیدار = meeting, او = him). نیا را نیاز places نیاز 'supplication/entreaty' as a definite object marked by را, with نیا identified in the clause (here functioning as the person who offers or is the object of the supplication). The whole segment sets the scene: 'to meet him, Nia (or the person named نیا) offered/was in supplication.' The precise agent/patient relation is determined by the surrounding narrative, but grammatically نیا را نیاز is a marked object phrase with نیاز as the noun 'supplication'.

sentences.s178-s81: From the treasure they brought forth peridots; they brought many lofty gems.
sentences.s178-s82: He uttered abundant praise for Siavash, for Khosrow had no one like him in appearance.
sentences.s178-s83: From the flank the nobles departed: spahbeds, chiefs, and illustrious men.
sentences.s178-s84: They cried 'Hail to the king!'; everyone scattered gold and gems.
sentences.s178-s45: The courtiers departed at the king's command; a citadel rose up at that place.
sentences.s178-s46: Into the citadel entered that king of the freemen — Aba Pir, Goudarz, and the Keshvadegan.
sentences.s178-s47: He saw a city within that wide fortress, full of gardens, squares, iwans, and palaces.
sentences.s178-s48: When the light from that place was taken away, at once the fortress became invisible.
sentences.s178-s5: He wore a purple standard about him, and on his feet (inside) he had put on golden shoes.
sentences.s178-s6: The world-seeker sat upon the golden throne; a crown on his head and a mace in his hand.
sentences.s178-s7: Two yāreh of ruby and a necklace of gold — in the gold’s inside gems were set.
sentences.s178-s8: The host marched on in companies and ranks, so that from the horses’ hooves the ground became like a mountain.
sentences.s178-s61: Tūs himself, with the Kayanian banner, was marching along with drum and golden shoes.
sentences.s178-s62: He brought it and presented it before the sovereign; he kissed the earth and handed it over.
sentences.s178-s63: He said to him that this drum and the golden shoes are for the auspiciously-starred Kayanian banner.
sentences.s178-s64: Look among the army to see who is worthy—which champion among them is fit for the task.
sentences.s178-s57: All the chiefs, one by one, went forward with offerings, joyful toward the king.
sentences.s178-s58: Fariborz came before him with a company — an Iranian troop, stalwart as a mountain.
sentences.s178-s59: When he saw him, he descended from the golden throne and kissed the face of his father's brother.
sentences.s178-s60: He seated him on the king's golden throne — the king who was adorned with attendants and jeweled ornaments.
sentences.s178-s49: Khosrow ordered that at that site a single dome be built, reaching up to the black cloud.
sentences.s178-s50: Its length and breadth were ten komands, and around it were high arches.
sentences.s178-s51: From outside (they) brought two half—single Arabian steeds and placed Azargushasp (there).
sentences.s178-s52: Around it sat the mobeds, the stargazers/astrologers, and likewise the wise men.
sentences.s178-s85: Thus is the world's making and disposition: from one hand he binds, to another he gives.
sentences.s178-s86: We are harmed by this going into deception; at times up, and at times down.
sentences.s178-s87: If the heart can have joy, why not spend the time in happiness?
sentences.s178-s88: Rejoice in pleasure and give (to others) with kindness; do not set the day's beauty upon your own heart.
sentences.s178-s89: He gave you, and will also grant a child — a tree that will spring up from your root.
sentences.s178-s90: Do you not see that the treasury is full of desire, while the world is arrayed in goodness?
sentences.s178-s91: There is no scarcity in the generosity of the just; abundance is fitting — do not grieve.
sentences.s178-s25: Khosrow took up a spear in his hand and fastened that very letter to the head of the spear.
sentences.s178-s26: He then set it up straight like a banner; in the world he wished for nothing except the divine farr of God.
sentences.s178-s27: He commanded that Giv, with a scorching spear, advance near it and charge.
sentences.s178-s28: He said to him, 'Take this admonitory letter to the high wall of the fortress.'
sentences.s178-s65: He offered excuses for his words and turned away from that futile counsel of his.
sentences.s178-s66: The victorious sovereign struck him, laughed, and seated him upon the throne.
sentences.s178-s67: He said to him: 'This is the Kaviani banner of vengeance, and that (is) the champion, and the golden shoes.'
sentences.s178-s68: I see no one deserving in the army; this deed and this arrangement befit you.
sentences.s178-s9: When he drew near the fortress, he sat up; he put on his armor and fastened the girth.
sentences.s178-s10: He summoned a scribe to the saddle; a certain man composed a letter beginning with 'Afarin.'
sentences.s178-s11: They wrote (it) on the flank with amber; such was the royal letter.
sentences.s178-s12: that this letter was from the servant of the Creator, the world-seeking, famed Kay Khosrow.
sentences.s178-s41: Many of the divs were slain by arrows; many a Zohreh was crushed and fell to the earth.
sentences.s178-s43: The world became like the shining moon; by the name, the sovereign victorious king.
sentences.s178-s44: A wind then arose, a single wind with a cry of 'Afarin'; the air became cheerful and the face of the earth smiled.
sentences.s178-s17: The whole world throughout is mine as kingship; from the Bull to the Fish (from Taurus to Pisces) it is mine.
sentences.s178-s18: If this fortress and land belong to Ahriman, then the world's Creator is an enemy (in the heart).
sentences.s178-s19: By (your) glory and by the command of the pure God, I will (throughout) smite them down with the mace to the earth.
sentences.s178-s20: And if the magicians' device is true, my own army must not be handed over to magic.
sentences.s178-s33: That famed letter vanished; a roar arose and the fortress's dust was carried off.
sentences.s178-s34: At that very moment, by the command of the pure God, a fissure opened in the fortress.
sentences.s178-s35: You said that it was the thunder-time of spring; a roar came from the plain and from the mountain.
sentences.s178-s36: The world turned like the black face of a Zangi — whether from the fortress's blast or from the dust of the host.
sentences.s178-s21: When I cast two coils of the lasso, I will bring the magicians' heads into fetters.
sentences.s178-s22: And if the blessed Soroush himself is present, there is, by God's command, a single host.
sentences.s178-s23: I am not in Ahriman's power; for my soul and body have been exalted by (that) glory.
sentences.s178-s24: By the command of God this empty (thing) acts, for this is the covenant of kingship.
sentences.s178-s1: When news came to the nobles and reached the old man Goudarz, the keshvadagān.
sentences.s178-s2: So Tous and Fariburz turned back; they could not go to the high fortress.
sentences.s178-s3: They adorned the elephants and a tumult rose; a fresh host of the sovereign arrived.
sentences.s178-s4: They placed a golden, peridot‑inlaid throne on the elephant and fastened the load.

sentences.s178-s30: Giv took up the spear in his hand, his soul full of cries of 'afarīn' — a God-worshipper.
sentences.s178-s31: When he set the letter upon the fortress wall, he inscribed (it) with the name ‘the world-seeker, Khosrow of lineage’.
sentences.s178-s32: He remembered the good gift from the Creator; then he set that swift charger in motion like the wind.
sentences.s178-s77: From there they again went toward the palace, and before the sovereign's throne a rustic tune was played.
sentences.s178-s78: When Kavus sat upon the golden throne, he then took Khosrow by the hand.
sentences.s178-s79: He brought him and set him in his place; from the treasury he demanded that the Kian crown be brought before him.
